originalTitle: South Sudan and Mauritius impose electricity curbs as Iran war triggers fuel fears in Africa
title: Lights On, Lights Off: Why Some African Countries Are Saving Power!
content:
Hello, amazing children of Africa! Your friendly news reporter is here to tell you about something important happening in some parts of our big, beautiful continent. Do you love watching cartoons, playing on your tablet, or seeing the lights come on in your home when it gets dark? All these cool things need something called *electricity* to work! Sometimes, grown-ups in some places like South Sudan and Mauritius are asking everyone to save electricity. This means the lights might go off for a little while, or you might not be able to charge your phone right away. Don't worry, it's not scary! It's just a way for everyone to share. Let's find out why!

What is Fuel?

Imagine your mum or dad's car. What does it drink to go places? Yes, it drinks something called *fuel*! Fuel is like super-food for machines. Big buses, loud trucks, and even the giant machines that make electricity for our homes, schools, and hospitals – they all need fuel to work. Without fuel, these machines can't move, and they can't make electricity. Fuel often looks like a thick, dark liquid. It helps things run! Just like you need food to play and learn, machines need fuel to do their jobs. Did you know? Many things we use every day, like making plastic toys or even cooking some foods, need fuel to happen at some point!

Where Do We Get Fuel From?

Fuel, like the kind that cars drink, comes from very, very deep under the ground. Clever people called miners dig deep into the earth to find it. Once they find it, it travels a long, long way in big ships or through long pipes to reach different countries all over the world, including our lovely African countries. It's a big journey for this special liquid! Sometimes, this fuel comes from faraway lands, across big oceans. When it arrives, it's used to power things or to help make electricity. Did you know? Our Earth has many wonderful treasures hidden deep inside it, not just fuel, but also shiny metals and beautiful gems!

A Problem Far Away

Sometimes, big problems happen in faraway places, like a big argument or a fight between countries. Right now, there's a difficult situation happening in a faraway land. We don't need to know all the grown-up details, but what's important for us to understand is that this big problem is making it harder for the big ships carrying fuel to travel safely and easily. It's like a traffic jam, but for huge ships carrying fuel! Because of this traffic jam, it's harder and slower for enough fuel to reach everyone who needs it. When less fuel arrives, it means there's less fuel for the big machines that make our electricity. This is why countries need to be extra careful with the fuel they have. Did you know? Even if a problem starts far away, it can sometimes make waves and affect people all around the world, like how ripples spread in a pond!

South Sudan and Mauritius: Saving Electricity

Two amazing countries in Africa, South Sudan and Mauritius, are seeing this problem. South Sudan is a big country in East Africa, known for its mighty Nile River. Mauritius is a beautiful island country, surrounded by clear blue ocean, off the coast of Africa. Both these places are full of wonderful people and exciting wildlife. Because less fuel is arriving, the grown-ups in charge in South Sudan and Mauritius are asking everyone to save electricity. They are doing something called 'electricity curbs'. This means that for a few hours each day, some lights might go off, or some machines might not run. They do this to make sure that everyone still gets *some* electricity, and that they don't run out completely. It's like sharing a small cake so everyone gets a slice! Did you know? South Sudan is home to amazing animals like elephants and giraffes! And Mauritius is famous for its dodo bird, even though it's not around anymore, it's a famous symbol!

Why Save Electricity?

When there isn't enough fuel to make all the electricity everyone usually uses, we have to be smart. If we just kept using electricity as usual, we might run out completely! And that would be a very big problem. Imagine if your fridge stopped working for days, or your school lights couldn't turn on for a whole week. By saving electricity, turning it off for a short time in different places, these countries are making sure that everyone still gets electricity for some parts of the day. It's a fair way to share what little fuel they have. It helps prevent a 'shortage', which just means 'not enough of something'. People might also feel a little 'concerned' or worried, but saving power helps to make things better. Did you know? Even turning off just one light bulb when you leave a room can help save energy for everyone!

What Happens When There's Not Enough?

When there's a 'shortage' of fuel, it means there's not enough to go around for everything. This can cause 'concerns', which means people might feel a bit worried or unsure about what will happen. For example, hospitals need electricity all the time for important machines that help sick people. Schools need light for children to learn. Homes need power for lights and to keep food cold. So, if there's not enough electricity, things can get tricky. Food in fridges might get warm, it might be hard to read or do homework after dark, and maybe your favourite TV show might not be on. But by saving power, countries try to keep the most important things working and make sure everyone still gets some light and power every day. Did you know? Some places use big batteries to store electricity, so they can use it later when they need it most!

How Can You Help?

You, our amazing young African champions, can help too! It's very simple. Here are some things you can do: * **Turn off lights:** When you leave a room, make sure to switch off the lights. No need for lights in an empty room! * **Unplug chargers:** When your phone or tablet is fully charged, unplug the charger. It still uses a tiny bit of electricity even if the device isn't plugged in! * **Turn off the TV:** If no one is watching the television, switch it off. Maybe you can go outside and play! * **Close the fridge door quickly:** Don't leave the fridge door open for too long. The cold air escapes, and the fridge has to work harder to get cold again. These small actions might seem tiny, but when many, many people do them, they add up to a huge help! You are helping your community save precious electricity. Did you know? The sun gives us amazing light every day! If you can, open curtains and use natural sunlight instead of electric lights during the day.

What Are Grown-Ups Doing?

Grown-ups are very busy working to solve this problem! They are looking for new ways to get fuel from different places so that they don't have to rely on just one source. They are also trying to find other ways to make electricity that don't need this kind of fuel. For example, some countries are building big machines that use the wind to make electricity (called wind turbines), or they are using special panels that collect sunlight to make electricity (called solar panels). They are also talking to other countries to try and make sure the big problem in the faraway land gets solved so that fuel can travel safely again. Everyone is working together to find the best solutions. Did you know? Africa has some of the best sunshine in the world, which is perfect for making electricity using solar panels!

Africa is Strong!

Even when things get a little difficult, Africa is full of strong, smart, and kind people. We always find ways to help each other and solve problems together. We are a continent of innovators and friendly neighbours. We will keep working hard, supporting each other, and finding smart ways to power our homes and schools. This challenge is a chance for us all to be a little smarter about how we use energy. We can learn and grow from it, making our communities even stronger and more resilient. Did you know? There are 54 amazing countries in Africa, each with its own special cultures, languages, and stories! So, remember, kids: saving electricity is a superhero power you have! By being smart with switches and plugs, you are helping South Sudan, Mauritius, and all of Africa shine brightly. Keep learning, keep playing, and keep being the wonderful, helpful children you are!
summary: Some African countries like South Sudan and Mauritius are asking people to save electricity because a faraway problem makes it hard for fuel to reach them, and fuel is needed to make power.
whyMatters: This is important because less electricity means less light, less charging for phones, and less power for schools and hospitals, but we can all help by saving energy.

originalTitle: Army commander convicted of Guinea stadium massacre dies in prison
title: Sad News from a Country Called Guinea
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends across Africa! Your friendly news reporter here. Today, we have some important news from a beautiful country in West Africa called Guinea. It's a bit of a serious story, but I will tell you about it in a way that is easy to understand. We talk about it so we can learn and hope for a better future where everyone is safe.

A Man Named Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité

Long, long ago, in a year called 2009, there was a man named Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité. He was a very important leader in the army in Guinea at that time. An army is a group of people who are meant to protect their country and its people. But sometimes, even important leaders make very bad choices. This man, Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité, was found guilty of doing something very, very wrong. When someone is found guilty, it means a judge and a court of law decided that they did a bad thing. This is called being 'convicted'. He was convicted because of a very sad event that happened many years ago.

What Happened Long Ago in Guinea?

Imagine a very big sports ground, like a football stadium. Many, many years ago, in 2009, lots of people gathered in a stadium in Guinea. They were there for a peaceful meeting. They wanted to share their ideas and hopes for their country. It should have been a safe and happy day. But something terrible happened on that day. It was a very, very sad day. Many people got hurt, and sadly, more than 150 people died. This kind of very sad and wrong event, where many people are hurt or killed, is sometimes called a 'massacre'. It was a dark day for Guinea, and it made many people very sad. Did you know? Guinea is a beautiful country in West Africa, and it's famous for its rivers and mountains! It has a long coastline along the Atlantic Ocean.

Finding Justice After a Sad Event

When something so bad happens, people want to know why, and they want to make sure that the people who did wrong are held responsible. This is called 'justice'. Justice means trying to make things fair and right again, even after something terrible has happened. The leaders in Guinea, and the brave people who were hurt or lost loved ones, worked for many, many years to find out what really happened. They wanted to make sure that the people who caused all that sadness were found and judged fairly. It took a very long time, like many years, for the court to listen to all the stories and gather all the facts. It was like a very, very long school project, but much more serious! They wanted to be sure they found the truth.

What Does 'Convicted' Mean?

After all those years, the court, which is like a very serious group of judges, made a decision. They decided that Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité and some other people were responsible for the sad things that happened that day. They were 'convicted', which means they were found guilty of their part in the bad event. When someone is convicted, it means they broke important rules and laws, and there are consequences. Just like when you break a rule at school, you might have to sit out of playtime. But for grown-ups who do very bad things, the consequences are much more serious. Did you know? A stadium is a very big place, sometimes with thousands of seats, where people can watch sports like football or listen to music concerts!

Life in Prison and Recent News

Because Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité was convicted, he was sent to prison. A prison is a special place where people who have been found guilty of serious crimes must stay. It is a place to make sure they cannot harm others and to think about the wrong things they did. Just recently, we heard some news. Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité, the man we are talking about, died while he was in prison. He was not a young man anymore. People, no matter where they are, can get sick and pass away. This is a part of life. He passed away while he was serving his time in prison for the sad events of 2009.

Why is This News Important for Africa?

This story might sound sad, but it's also a story about hope and fairness. It shows that in countries like Guinea, and all over Africa, there are important rules and laws. And everyone, even powerful army leaders, must follow these rules. It tells us that if someone does something very wrong, like hurting many people, they will be judged, and there will be consequences. This is how countries try to make sure everyone is safe and that justice is served. It's about making things right for the people who were hurt and for their families. Did you know? Many countries in Africa are working very hard to build peace and make sure everyone lives safely and happily! This news helps us remember the people who were hurt in 2009. It also reminds us that even when bad things happen, people in Africa keep working for justice and for a peaceful future. We should all remember to be kind to one another and always choose to do the right thing. That way, we can all help make our world a happier and safer place for everyone. Thank you for listening!
summary: A former army commander in Guinea, Aboubacar Sidiki Diakité, who was convicted for a terrible event in 2009 where many people were hurt or died, recently passed away while in prison.
whyMatters: This news is important because it shows that even powerful people must follow laws and face consequences if they do very wrong things, helping to make sure countries like Guinea work for justice and a safer future for everyone.

originalTitle: Senegal to parade Afcon trophy despite title being stripped
title: Senegal's Big Celebration: A Trophy Parade!
content:
Hello, Future Superstars!

Hi everyone! It's your friendly African news reporter here, ready to tell you about some exciting news from our amazing continent. We love sports, especially football, right? Today, we're talking about a country called Senegal. It's a beautiful country in West Africa, known for its lovely people and, of course, its fantastic football team! Their football team is very famous. They are called the 'Lions of Teranga'. 'Teranga' is a special word in Senegal that means hospitality and being welcoming. Isn't that nice?

What is AFCON?

Have you ever heard of something called AFCON? It sounds a bit like 'African'! That's because it stands for the 'Africa Cup of Nations'. AFCON is a super big football competition. It's like a huge party where all the best football teams from different countries in Africa come together. They play many exciting games to see who is the very best team in Africa. Everyone watches, cheers, and gets very excited! It's a time when we celebrate African talent and teamwork. Many kids dream of playing in AFCON one day. Did you know? The first AFCON tournament happened way back in 1957! That was a very long time ago.

Who are the Brave Lions of Senegal?

Senegal's football team, the Lions of Teranga, are very strong and play with lots of heart. They have amazing players who run fast, kick hard, and score fantastic goals. They worked very, very hard in a special AFCON competition. This competition was for players who play in African clubs. And guess what? They won! They were the champions of that AFCON tournament for local players! Everyone in Senegal was so, so happy and proud. Winning meant they got a super shiny trophy. A trophy is like a big, fancy cup or shield that shows everyone you are the winner. They brought the trophy home to Senegal, and people danced in the streets! It was a truly joyful time for the whole country.

A Shiny Trophy and a Big Hug

Imagine winning a big prize after working so hard! That's how the players felt when they lifted the AFCON trophy. The trophy is a symbol of their hard work, their teamwork, and their big win for Senegal. It shows that when you practice a lot and play together as friends, you can achieve amazing things. Winning this trophy made all the children in Senegal want to play football even more. It inspires them to chase their dreams! Did you know? A football match has two teams, and each team tries to kick a ball into the other team's net to score a goal!

Parading the Trophy!

Now, here's the exciting part of our news! Even though some grown-ups are having a bit of a disagreement about the trophy, Senegal wants to celebrate! They are going to have a special 'parade'. A parade is like a big party where people walk or ride in vehicles, showing off something special. Senegal's football team wants to show off their beautiful trophy. They want everyone to see it and remember their big win. This parade won't be in Senegal this time. It will be far away in a big city called Paris, in a country called France. They are going to parade the trophy just before they play a 'friendly match' there. A friendly match is a game just for fun and practice, not for points in a big competition. They will play against a team from a country called Peru. Peru is very far away, across the big ocean! So, even if they are far from home, they want to show their joy and their trophy to the world.

A Bit of a Puzzle

Now, here's the part that's a bit like a puzzle for grown-ups. The boss of the football group in Senegal says that some other grown-ups have made a decision they don't agree with. It's about the trophy they won. He feels like someone tried to take their win away, like a 'robbery' of their joy. This makes him and many Senegalese people feel a little sad and confused. Another country, Morocco, was given the title for the tournament. This decision makes the people in Senegal feel like their original victory isn't being fully recognised by everyone. But here's the good news: Even with this puzzle, the Senegalese team is still very, very proud of what they did. They still feel like they are champions, because they played their best and won fair and square on the field.

Why Are They Still Happy?

Because they believe in themselves! They know they played wonderfully and won that shiny trophy with their own hard work. They want to show everyone that they are still champions in their hearts, no matter what some grown-ups decide. This parade is like saying, 'Look what we did! We are proud of our team and our country!' It's a way for them to share their happiness and pride with their fans, even if there's a little bit of a disagreement happening. It teaches us that even when things get tricky, we can still celebrate our successes and be proud of our efforts. Did you know? The football is usually black and white or other bright colors, so players can see it easily on the green grass!

Playing Fair is Key!

In football, and in life, playing fair is super important. Everyone should follow the rules. Sometimes, grown-ups have different ideas about what is fair. That's what is happening here with the trophy. But what matters most is that we learn to play with respect, try our best, and shake hands at the end, even if we don't always agree. The Senegalese team shows us that even when things are not perfect, we can still stand tall and celebrate what we believe is right. It's a good lesson for all of us, isn't it?

Fun Facts About Football!

* Did you know? Football is the most popular sport in the whole wide world! So many people love to play and watch it. * A football game lasts for 90 minutes, with a short break in the middle. That's a lot of running! * The person who stops the ball from going into the net is called the goalkeeper. They wear special gloves. * Every team has a captain, who is like the leader on the field. They help their teammates.

Let's Cheer for Senegal!

So, let's all cheer for the brave Lions of Teranga! They are showing great spirit and pride. They remind us that believing in yourself and celebrating your hard work is always important. Let's hope they have a wonderful parade in Paris and a fun friendly game. Go, Senegal, go! Thank you for listening to our African news for kids today. See you next time!
summary: Senegal's football team, the Lions of Teranga, will parade their AFCON trophy in Paris before a friendly match, even though some grown-ups are disagreeing about their title.
whyMatters: This is important because it shows how a team can be proud and celebrate their hard work, even when there are disagreements, inspiring children across Africa.

originalTitle: Pakistan says it is mediating US-Iran talks
title: Helping Friends Talk: Big Countries Want Peace!
content:
Hello From Africa!

Hello, hello, my amazing little news detectives from sunny Africa! It's your friendly reporter here, ready to share some super important news with you. Are your listening ears on? Good! Today, we're talking about something very special: Countries trying to be friends. Just like sometimes you and your friend might have a little disagreement, big countries can also have big disagreements.

What's the Big News?

Guess what? There's a country far away called Pakistan, and it's doing something really cool! Pakistan is helping two other big countries, the USA and Iran, talk to each other. Why are they talking? To try and stop a big problem called a 'war'. A war is when countries fight with each other, and it makes many people very, very sad.

What is a War?

Imagine if your toy car broke, and instead of asking for help or trying to fix it, you just started shouting and throwing things. That's a bit like a tiny, tiny war. But a real war is much, much bigger and scarier. When countries are in a war, soldiers fight, and sometimes, even innocent people get hurt. Homes can be broken, and schools might close. It's not a happy time at all. That's why everyone wants wars to stop, and for 'peace' to come instead.

Did you know that 'peace' means when everyone is happy and safe, and there is no fighting? It's like when you play nicely with your friends in the playground!

Talking is Better Than Fighting!

Just like when you have a problem with a friend, what's the best thing to do? Talk about it, right? You tell them how you feel, and they tell you how they feel. And then you try to find a way to make things better. Well, big countries need to do the same thing! When countries have problems, it's always best for them to talk, talk, talk. Not fight.

How Does Pakistan Help?

This is where Pakistan comes in, like a super-friendly helper! Pakistan is acting like a 'mediator'. What does that mean? Imagine if two of your friends, let's call them Sarah and Tom, are not talking to each other. Maybe they had a misunderstanding. You, as a good friend, could go to Sarah and say, "Tom is feeling sad, maybe you should talk." Then you go to Tom and say, "Sarah wants to understand what happened." You don't take sides, you just help them send messages and listen to each other. Pakistan is doing something like that for the USA and Iran.

What are 'Indirect Talks'?

Now, these talks are 'indirect'. That means the USA and Iran are not sitting in the same room, face-to-face, just yet. It's a bit like sending notes back and forth through a helper. Pakistan is helping to carry those messages. This way, they can start to understand each other better, without getting angry again. It's a very careful way to start making friends again.

Who are the USA and Iran?

The USA is a very big country far, far away. And Iran is another big country, also quite far from us here in Africa. They are both important countries, and when they are not fighting, it's good for everyone in the world. Think of them like two very important neighbours who need to learn to get along for the whole street to be happy.

Why is This Important for All of Us?

You might think, "But I live in Africa! Why does this news matter to me?" Well, when big countries fight, it can make the whole world feel a bit shaky. It can make things difficult for other countries too. Like when there's a big storm far away, we might still feel some of its effects. But when countries talk and make peace, it makes the whole world a safer and happier place for everyone, including you and me! It means more kindness, more helping, and more smiles all around the globe.

Did you know that the United Nations is an organization where almost all countries in the world come together to talk and solve problems peacefully? It's like a giant school assembly for countries!

Pakistan's Good Heart

So, Pakistan is showing a really good heart by helping the USA and Iran talk. They want to see peace, just like we all do. It takes courage and kindness to help others sort out their problems, especially big ones like disagreements between countries. We should all be happy when countries choose to talk instead of fight. It's a sign that they want to build bridges, not walls.

Let's Hope for Peace!

So, let's clap for Pakistan for being a good friend and helper! And let's hope that the USA and Iran keep talking and find a way to be peaceful neighbours. Remember, talking is always the best way to solve problems, whether you're a little kid or a big country! Keep shining bright, my little African stars! And keep listening for more happy news about peace and friendship around the world!
summary: Pakistan is helping two big countries, the USA and Iran, talk to each other to help stop a war and bring peace.
whyMatters: When countries talk instead of fight, it makes the whole world a safer and happier place for everyone, including children like us in Africa.

originalTitle: 'A game-changing moment for social media' - what next for big tech after landmark addiction verdict?
title: Super Apps and Super Safe Fun! Big News for Our Screens!
content:
Hello, Fellow Explorers!

Hi friends! I'm Ama, your kid-friendly news reporter from right here in beautiful Africa! Today, we have some super interesting news that's buzzing all the way from a far-off place called America. It's all about the apps many of us love to use on our phones and tablets – like TikTok, WhatsApp, or even watching fun videos on YouTube! We know these apps can be super fun, but sometimes, they can be a bit tricky too. This big news could mean some cool changes for how these apps work, making them even better and safer for everyone, especially for us kids here in Africa and all over the world!

What Are These "Social Media" Apps?

Let's start with the basics! What exactly are "social media" apps? Well, they are like super cool playgrounds, but instead of swings and slides, you share pictures, videos, and messages with your friends and family. You can watch funny dances, learn new things, or even talk to your grandmother who lives far away. Apps like WhatsApp let you send messages to your friends. TikTok lets you watch and make short, fun videos. Instagram is for sharing photos. They help us connect and have fun! Did you know? Many people in Africa use their phones to connect with family and friends, even in different villages or countries! It's a wonderful way to stay in touch.

Feeling Super Sticky?

Have you ever had a lollypop or a piece of candy that was so yummy, you just wanted to eat more and more, even when your tummy started to feel a bit full? Or maybe you were playing a game that was so exciting, you just couldn't put it down? Sometimes, apps can be a bit like that. They are made to be super fun and engaging, so much so that it's hard to stop using them. When something makes you want to do it all the time, even when you know you should stop to do other important things like homework, playing outside, or sleeping, grown-ups call that feeling "addiction." It means you really, really want to keep doing it, and it's hard to stop by yourself. The clever people who make these apps sometimes use special tricks to make them extra "sticky" so we keep coming back.

A Big Decision Far Away!

So, what's the big news? Well, in America, some grown-ups who are like super important judges and lawyers looked at these big companies that make the apps. They made a very important decision, called a "verdict." This verdict says that some of these big companies might have made their apps *too* sticky. They might have used tricks that made people, especially young people, want to use the apps for too long, almost like getting stuck. Think of it like this: if you have a toy that's so much fun it makes you forget to eat your dinner, maybe that toy needs a little adjustment so you can still have fun but remember your healthy habits too! This big decision is saying that the app companies have a responsibility to make sure their apps are fun, but also safe and not *too* sticky.

Why Is This Big News For Us?

Even though this decision happened far away in America, it's super important for us here in Africa too! Why? Because many of the same apps are used by kids and families all across our continent. This decision means that the big companies might have to think harder about how they design their apps. They might have to change things so that the apps are still fun, but they don't make it so hard for us to take breaks and do other important things. It's like when your parents set rules about screen time – they do it because they love you and want you to have a healthy balance of playing, learning, and relaxing. This verdict is a grown-up way of saying, "Hey, app makers, let's make sure our apps help kids have a healthy balance!" Did you know? Africa has some of the fastest-growing numbers of internet users in the world! That's why making sure our online spaces are safe and healthy is extra important for our children.

What Might Change For Our Apps?

So, what could change? Well, the app companies might have to make some adjustments. Maybe they will: * **Give us more reminders to take a break.** Imagine the app saying, "Hey, Ama, you've been watching videos for a while! How about going outside for a bit?" * **Change how videos play automatically.** Sometimes, when one video finishes, another one starts right away. Maybe they will ask us first if we want to watch another one. * **Make it easier to find tools to limit screen time.** Your parents might be able to set timers right within the app more easily. * **Design them to be less colourful and exciting all the time.** Sometimes too many bright colours and constant notifications can make it hard to look away. They might make them calmer. These changes could help us have fun with apps without getting stuck for too long. It's about finding a good balance!

How Can We Stay Safe And Have Fun?

While these big changes might take some time, there are super important things we can do RIGHT NOW to have fun with apps safely: 1. **Talk to Your Parents:** Always tell your parents about the apps you use and ask them for help if something makes you feel worried or uncomfortable. They are your best helpers! 2. **Set Screen Time Rules:** Work with your parents to decide how much time you spend on screens each day. When the time is up, put the device away and find other fun things to do. 3. **Take Breaks:** Even if you're allowed to play, take little breaks! Stand up, stretch, look out the window, or go get a drink of water. 4. **Balance is Key:** Remember that there's a whole world of fun beyond the screen! Playing outside, reading books, drawing, helping with chores, and talking to your family are all super important.

Lots of Fun Beyond Screens!

Think about all the amazing things you can do when you're not on a screen! You could: * Kick a ball with your friends outside. * Read an exciting storybook. * Draw a picture of your favourite animal. * Help your mum or dad in the garden. * Play traditional African games like 'Mancala' or 'Morabaraba'. * Tell stories with your family under the stars. * Sing songs and dance! Did you know? Playing outside helps your body grow strong and makes your brain sharper! It's like magic!

Keeping Our African Kids Safe Online

This news is a good reminder for all of us here in Africa about being smart and safe with technology. Our phones and tablets are amazing tools that can help us learn, play, and stay connected. But just like a sharp knife can help cut vegetables but needs to be used carefully, our apps need to be used wisely. We should always remember not to talk to strangers online, not to share personal information like our home address, and always tell a trusted adult if something feels wrong.

Our Bright Future With Tech!

So, what does all this mean for our future? It means that we are moving towards a world where technology is not just exciting, but also designed to keep us healthy and happy. It's about having the best of both worlds – amazing apps for learning and fun, and plenty of time for playing, exploring, and growing strong bodies and minds. Let's look forward to a future where our apps are super fun AND super safe for every kid in Africa and around the globe! Stay curious, stay safe, and keep exploring the amazing world around you, both online and offline!
summary: A big decision in America means that the companies making popular apps like TikTok and WhatsApp might need to change them to be less "sticky" and more balanced, making them safer for kids everywhere, including in Africa.
whyMatters: This is important because it means the apps we use could become safer and help us have fun without getting stuck on them for too long, encouraging a healthy balance between screen time and playing outside.

originalTitle: French court hands Islam scholar Tariq Ramadan 18-year jail term for rape
title: French court hands Islam scholar Tariq Ramadan 18-year jail term for rape
content: Ramadan, a 63-year-old former Oxford professor, denied the charges but his lawyers said he was too ill to attend the trial.

originalTitle: 'Not for us to ride the rollercoaster' - what Wales must do to win World Cup play-off
title: 'Not for us to ride the rollercoaster' - what Wales must do to win World Cup play-off
content: As Wales host Bosnia-Herzegovina in a World Cup play-off semi-final, head coach Craig Bellamy explains what his side must do to win.

originalTitle: PSG's league game between legs of Liverpool Champions League tie postponed
title: PSG's league game between legs of Liverpool Champions League tie postponed
content: Paris St-Germain's Ligue One match against Lens, which was set to take place between the two legs of their Champions League quarter-final against Liverpool, has been postponed.

originalTitle: Rodri open to Real Madrid move despite Atletico past
title: Rodri open to Real Madrid move despite Atletico past
content: Manchester City midfielder Rodri says "you can't turn down the best clubs in the world" as he refuses to rule out a move to Real Madrid.
